title: If You Want Animals to Understand You, Speak Slowly
gist: A PLOS Biology study of over 2,000 recordings from 98 species argues nearly all animal vocal communication shares a slow ~2.7 Hz rhythm, an ancient pace set by how fast a brain can process sound structure.

insights.claim: Across mammals, birds, amphibians, insects, reptiles, and fish, nearly all animal vocal communication shares a rhythm of about 2.7 hertz, roughly three vocalizations per second.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: A striking cross-species constant in biology.
insights.quote: a singular rhythm, of around 2.7 hertz, or about three vocalizations per second

insights.claim: Scientists propose animals adopted this ~2.7 Hz rhythm hundreds of millions of years ago, before mammals emerged, because it is the speed at which a brain best processes the structure of sounds.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Roots communication timing in neural processing limits, not chance.
insights.locator: A team of scientists from Switzerland

insights.claim: The researchers propose brains analyze sound in two complementary modes: slow delta-band oscillations (1-4 Hz) track sequence and structure, while timbre and pitch (which vary with body size) identify individual speakers.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: A dual-mode model of auditory processing applicable across species.
insights.locator: To analyze sound, animal and human brains

insights.claim: The rhythm finding is based on more than 2,000 sound recordings produced by 98 species, published in PLOS Biology.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Breadth of the dataset lends weight to the universality claim.
insights.locator: which is the rhythm they found
